lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Gallop apace, you fiery-footed steeds,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Towards Phoebus’ lodging: such a waggoner

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: As Phaethon would whip you to the west,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And bring in cloudy night immediately.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Spread thy close curtain, love-performing night,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That runaways’ eyes may wink, and Romeo

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Leap to these arms, untalk’d of and unseen.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Lovers can see to do their amorous rites

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: By their own beauties; or, if love be blind,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: It best agrees with night. Come, civil night,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Thou sober-suited matron, all in black,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And learn me how to lose a winning match,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Play’d for a pair of stainless maidenhoods:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Hood my unmann’d blood, bating in my cheeks,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: With thy black mantle; till strange love, grown bold,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Think true love acted simple modesty.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Come, night; come, Romeo; come, thou day in night;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For thou wilt lie upon the wings of night

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Whiter than new snow on a raven’s back.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Come, gentle night, come, loving, black-brow’d night,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Give me my Romeo; and, when he shall die,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Take him and cut him out in little stars,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And he will make the face of heaven so fine

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That all the world will be in love with night

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And pay no worship to the garish sun.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, I have bought the mansion of a love,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But not possess’d it, and, though I am sold,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Not yet enjoy’d: so tedious is this day

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: As is the night before some festival

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To an impatient child that hath new robes

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And may not wear them. O, here comes my nurse,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And she brings news; and every tongue that speaks

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But Romeo’s name speaks heavenly eloquence.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Ay, ay, the cords.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Ay me! what news? why dost thou wring thy hands?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Ah, well-a-day! he’s dead, he’s dead, he’s dead!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: We are undone, lady, we are undone!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Alack the day! he’s gone, he’s kill’d, he’s dead!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Can heaven be so envious?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Romeo can,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Though heaven cannot: O Romeo, Romeo!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Who ever would have thought it? Romeo!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What devil art thou, that dost torment me thus?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: This torture should be roar’d in dismal hell.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Hath Romeo slain himself? say thou but “I,”

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And that bare vowel “I” shall poison more

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Than the death-darting eye of cockatrice:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I am not I, if there be such an I;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or those eyes shut, that make thee answer “I.”

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: If he be slain, say “I”; or if not, no:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Brief sounds determine of my weal or woe.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I saw the wound, I saw it with mine eyes—

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: God save the mark!—here on his manly breast:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: A piteous corse, a bloody piteous corse;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Pale, pale as ashes, all bedaub’d in blood,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: All in gore-blood; I swounded at the sight.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, break, my heart! poor bankrupt, break at once!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To prison, eyes, ne’er look on liberty!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Vile earth, to earth resign; end motion here;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And thou and Romeo press one heavy bier!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O Tybalt, Tybalt, the best friend I had!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O courteous Tybalt! honest gentleman!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: That ever I should live to see thee dead!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What storm is this that blows so contrary?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Is Romeo slaughter’d, and is Tybalt dead?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: My dear-loved cousin, and my dearer lord?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Then, dreadful trumpet, sound the general doom!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For who is living, if those two are gone?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Tybalt is gone, and Romeo banished;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Romeo that kill’d him, he is banished.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O God! did Romeo’s hand shed Tybalt’s blood?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: It did, it did; alas the day, it did!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O serpent heart, hid with a flowering face!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Did ever dragon keep so fair a cave?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Beautiful tyrant! fiend angelical!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Dove-feather’d raven! wolvish-ravening lamb!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Despised substance of divinest show!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Just opposite to what thou justly seem’st,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: A damned saint, an honourable villain!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O nature, what hadst thou to do in hell,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: When thou didst bower the spirit of a fiend

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: In moral paradise of such sweet flesh?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Was ever book containing such vile matter

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: So fairly bound? O, that deceit should dwell

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: In such a gorgeous palace!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: There’s no trust,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: No faith, no honesty in men; all perjured,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: All forsworn, all naught, all dissemblers.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Ah, where’s my man? give me some aqua vitae:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: These griefs, these woes, these sorrows make me old.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Shame come to Romeo!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Blister’d be thy tongue

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For such a wish! he was not born to shame:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Upon his brow shame is ashamed to sit;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For ’tis a throne where honour may be crown’d

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Sole monarch of the universal earth.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, what a beast was I to chide at him!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Will you speak well of him that kill’d your cousin?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Shall I speak ill of him that is my husband?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Ah, poor my lord, what tongue shall smooth thy name,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: When I, thy three-hours wife, have mangled it?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But, wherefore, villain, didst thou kill my cousin?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That villain cousin would have kill’d my husband:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Back, foolish tears, back to your native spring;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Your tributary drops belong to woe,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Which you, mistaking, offer up to joy.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: My husband lives, that Tybalt would have slain;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And Tybalt’s dead, that would have slain my husband:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: All this is comfort; wherefore weep I then?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Some word there was, worser than Tybalt’s death,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That murder’d me: I would forget it fain;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But, O, it presses to my memory,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Like damned guilty deeds to sinners’ minds:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: “Tybalt is dead, and Romeo—banished;”

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That “banished,” that one word “banished,”

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Hath slain ten thousand Tybalts. Tybalt’s death

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Was woe enough, if it had ended there:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or, if sour woe delights in fellowship

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And needly will be rank’d with other griefs,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Why follow’d not, when she said “Tybalt’s dead,”

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: “Thy father,” or “thy mother,” nay, or both,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Which modern lamentations might have moved?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But with a rearward following Tybalt’s death,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: “Romeo is banished,” to speak that word,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Is father, mother, Tybalt, Romeo, Juliet,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: All slain, all dead. “Romeo is banished!”

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: There is no end, no limit, measure, bound,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: In that word’s death; no words can that woe sound.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Where is my father, and my mother, nurse?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Weeping and wailing over Tybalt’s corse:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Will you go to them? I will bring you thither.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Wash they his wounds with tears: mine shall be spent,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: When theirs are dry, for Romeo’s banishment.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Take up those cords: poor ropes, you are beguiled,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Both you and I; for Romeo is exiled:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: He made you for a highway to my bed;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But I, a maid, die maiden-widowed.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Come, cords, come, nurse; I’ll to my wedding-bed;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And death, not Romeo, take my maidenhead!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Hie to your chamber: I’ll find Romeo

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: To comfort you: I wot well where he is.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Hark ye, your Romeo will be here at night:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I’ll to him; he is hid at Laurence’ cell.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, find him! give this ring to my true knight,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And bid him come to take his last farewell.

stage_directions.text: Now, nurse, what news? What hast thou there? the cords That Romeo bid thee fetch?

the horses of the sun-god Phoebus, drawing his chariot
the sun-god's resting place — the west
Apollo's son who lost control of the sun-chariot; Juliet begs for just such recklessness
the night that enacts love — when marriages are consummated
runaway gossips; a long-debated crux — Juliet asks their eyes to close

the rites of love — the marriage-bed
by the light of their own radiance
sober, modest night; also civilized
a dignified married woman in dark dress — night personified as chaperone

glosses.phrase: learn me how to lose a winning match
glosses.definition: teach me how to win by surrender — the bridal paradox
spotless virginities
the shyness of the virgin bride
falconry metaphor — cover my unmastered blood with a hood, as with an untrained falcon
unbroken; untrained (of a falcon)
fluttering; beating wings — another falconry term
married love dressed as modest innocence

glosses.definition: Romeo's white purity against the dark of night

glosses.phrase: cut him out in little stars
glosses.definition: one of Shakespeare's most gorgeous lines — transforming the beloved into constellations
gaudy, showy daytime sun
have not yet enjoyed it — Juliet's sexual longing

rope ladder — the one Romeo will climb tonight
the Nurse's wailing misleads Juliet into thinking Romeo is dead
a mythical serpent whose glance kills; Juliet's one look will be lethal if Romeo is dead
a short 'yes' or 'no' will settle it
the Nurse describes Tybalt's body, not Romeo's
corpse
swooned; fainted
ruined; devoid of resources

glosses.phrase: O serpent heart, hid with a flowering face!
glosses.definition: Juliet's stacked oxymorons — Romeo the beautiful killer; the Petrarchan paradox turned tragic
black bird wearing dove-plumage — inversion of his 'dove among crows' at the ball
wolf disguised as lamb

glosses.phrase: Just opposite to what thou justly seem’st
glosses.definition: Romeo is the exact opposite of what he appears — moral deception
oxymoron; false holiness
oxymoron — the moral world has flipped
beauty housing treachery — Juliet on Romeo
vile; wicked
strong distilled spirits — the Nurse's restorative drink
Juliet rebukes her own tears — they belong to joy, not mourning
the word of catastrophe that overwhelms Juliet
common grief; lamentation in the ordinary way
depth

annotations.title: Juliet's epithalamium — 'Gallop apace, you fiery-footed steeds'
annotations.body: While the city is still reeling from 3.1, Juliet (who does not yet know) calls on night to come quickly so Romeo can reach her. Her speech is an epithalamium, a wedding-night hymn in the Renaissance tradition (Spenser's Epithalamion, 1595, is the English masterpiece). 'Come, civil night, / Thou sober-suited matron, all in black' — night as chaperone to her first sexual experience. The speech then pivots to sublime astronomy: 'Take him and cut him out in little stars, / And he will make the face of heaven so fine / That all the world will be in love with night.' The pitch of desire here is vertical, cosmic — and the audience, knowing Tybalt is dead and Romeo is about to be banished, receives the speech as both gorgeous and devastating.
sources: Heather Dubrow, A Happier Eden: The Politics of Marriage in the Stuart Epithalamium (1990)

annotations.title: Juliet's stacked oxymorons — the Petrarchan mode now turned tragic
annotations.body: When Juliet believes Romeo has killed Tybalt, she lets loose: 'O serpent heart, hid with a flowering face! / ... Dove-feather'd raven! wolvish-ravening lamb! / Despised substance of divinest show! / Just opposite to what thou justly seem'st, / A damned saint, an honourable villain!' These are the same oxymorons Romeo played with for Rosaline in 1.1 — but now they mean something. Rosaline-Petrarchism was pose; Juliet's paradoxes here are a genuine moral crisis, the lover discovering her beloved is also a killer. Shakespeare turns the rhetorical device from decoration to tragic utterance. This is the play asking: how do you speak when the person you love has done the thing you hate? The answer is oxymoron — the figure of impossible truth.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

trials.prompt: What Renaissance genre does Juliet's 'Gallop apace' speech belong to?
options: The epithalamium — a wedding-night hymn celebrating the approach of consummation

trials.prompt: Juliet's 'serpent heart, hid with a flowering face' and 'Dove-feather'd raven' are examples of —
options: Oxymoron — but now carrying real moral weight, unlike Romeo's earlier Petrarchan versions

trials.prompt: What is the effect of juxtaposing Juliet's 'Gallop apace' with what the audience knows (Tybalt's death, Romeo's banishment)?
options: Dramatic irony — the audience receives the speech as both exquisite and devastating; the ecstasy is already shadowed by the catastrophe
It creates confusion in the plot
